Guide.Description: The model provides a qualitative prediction of inhalation carcinogenic potency (expert assessment based on presence of carcinogenic effects in rodents). It is based on a classification and regression trees (CART) method and it is implemented inside the VEGA online platform, accessible at: http://www.vegahub.eu/.
Guide.Dataset:
The model has been developed using data from the Risk Assessment Information System (RAIS) Toxicity values database (https://rais.ornl.gov). Data cover different pollutants categories including organic and inorganic compounds such as dioxins, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs, pesticides and metals frequently found in contaminated sites. 990 values were retrieved for inhalation unit risk (IUR, µg/m3). Canonical SMILES were retrieved for each chemical from two sources (JChem for Office and ChemID plus) then chemicals showing incongruences between the various sources were rejected. Most of the QSAR models cannot handle inorganic compounds, metals and metal complexes, organic salts and data related to mixtures and these compounds have been also rejected. Ionized structures were neutralized and counterions eliminated. The datasets were further checked for the presence of duplicates. For the training/test splitting, constitutional and ring descriptors blocks have been used, together with the experimental class value, as input for a principal components analysis (PCA). The first principal component (PC) has been used to rank the compounds, then a venetian blind approach has been used to split training and test set compounds with an 80-20% ratio.
The training set contains 598 compounds. The test set counts 149 compounds.
Guide.Descriptors:
Classification and regression trees (CART). CART implementation in R (rpart package) Recursive partitioning for classification, regression and survival trees. An implementation of most of the functionality of the 1984 book by Breiman, Friedman, Olshen and Stone. The descriptors have been generated with VEGA software using CDK libraries. The model uses these descriptors: [1]piPC10 molecular multiple path count of order 10 [2]ATSC2p Centred Broto-Moreau autocorrelation of lag 2 weighted by polarizability [3]EEig15(bo) Eigenvalue based descriptor on Edge adjacency weighted by bond order [4]F01[N-N] Frequency of N - N at topological distance 1 [5]BEL3m Burden Eigenvalue Descriptors [6]GATS1v Geary autocorrelation of lag 1 weighted by van der Waals volume [7]PW4 path/walk 4 - Randic shape index [8]MATS3s Moran autocorrelation of lag 3 weighted by I-state [9]BEH4e Burden Eigenvalue Descriptors
An in-house tool developed in the R statistical platform has been used to select the best descriptors set and size to be employed for the final model. The approach was based on a forward selection technique: starting from the descriptor most correlated with the experimental data, at each iteration the descriptor leading to the best model (among all the available descriptors) was added, until the size of 25 descriptors. Models have been built with a Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) modelling and applied with a bootstrap cross-validation approach (n = 100 iterations). The fitness function has been calculated for each model as a linear combination of the mean values of the accuracy, sensitivity and specificity obtained from the models built in each bootstrap iteration. This function has been used to select the best descriptor to be added to proceed to the next iteration.

AD.Name: Similar_Molecules
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: strongly similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: only moderately similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: no similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found

AD.Name: Accuracy
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is good
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not optimal
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not adequate

AD.Name: Concordance
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that agree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that slightly disagree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that completely disagree with the target compound predicted value

AD.Name: Model_Descriptors_Range_Check
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: descriptors for this compound have values inside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: descriptors for this compound have values outside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set

AD.Name: ACF_SimCheck
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: all atom centered fragment of the compound have been found in the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: some atom centered fragment of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: a prominent number of atom centered fragments of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments

AD.Name: AD_Index
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: predicted substance is into the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: predicted substance could be out of the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: predicted substance is out of the the Applicability Domain of the model

xml.Reference: The model has been developed by Alberto Manganaro, Kode srl, info@kode-solutions.net, in 2017. It has been described here: Benfenati E, Roncaglioni A, Lombardo A, Manganaro A. Integrating QSAR, Read-Across, and Screening Tools: The VEGAHUB Platform as an Example. Advances in Computational Toxicology: Springer; 2019. p. 365-81. For a general description of the VEGA software see: Benfenati E, Manganaro A, Gini G VEGA-QSAR: AI inside a platform for predictive toxicology Proceedings of the workshop "Popularize Artificial Intelligence 2013", December 5th 2013, Turin, Italy Published on CEUR Workshop Proceedings Vol-1107
